title: Who Betrayed Tamil Nadu? Do You Understand Now?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Do You Understand Now?
Udanpirappē,

At an election campaign meeting in Karur, Jayalalithaa accused the DMK of continuously betraying Tamil Nadu on the Cauvery river-water issue. I have answered this accusation, which Chief Minister Jayalalithaa keeps repeating, many times. Yet whenever she can find nothing else to speak about, she immediately begins saying that “the DMK committed betrayal,” whether over the Cauvery, Katchatheevu or the Sri Lankan Tamil issue.
Replying to the debate on the Governor's Address on 8 February 2013, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa spoke at length in the Assembly about Cauvery water. I wrote a reply to it on 12 February 2013 itself.

She then spoke in the Assembly as though the Cauvery Water Disputes Tribunal's interim order had been secured through her unceasing efforts. It was while the DMK was in office that, for the first time, a letter was sent to the Union Government on 17 February 1970 seeking the establishment of a Cauvery tribunal. It was under a DMK government that the Legislative Assembly adopted a resolution on 8 July 1971 demanding its establishment.

Again, while the DMK was in office, an all-party leaders' meeting was held on 27 July 1989 and unanimously resolved to ask the Union Government to constitute the tribunal. Leaders of every party attended except the AIADMK led by Jayalalithaa. The extent of her interest in establishing the tribunal can be understood from the AIADMK's boycott of that all-party meeting.

After V.P. Singh became Prime Minister on 2 December 1989, I wrote to him seeking the tribunal's establishment. He asked me to have a resolution adopted in the Assembly and sent to him. Accordingly, the Assembly again resolved on 24 April 1990 that a tribunal was necessary. It was as a result of that resolution that the tribunal was constituted on 2 June 1990, while the DMK was in office and I was Chief Minister.

On 28 July 1990, it was the DMK government that petitioned the tribunal for an interim order. On 5 January 1991, the tribunal said it lacked the power to grant one. On 10 January 1991, the DMK government approached the Supreme Court, asking it to decide whether the tribunal possessed that power. On 26 April 1991, the Supreme Court ruled that it did. It was only because of the Supreme Court judgment sought and obtained by the DMK government that the tribunal issued its interim award on 25 June 1991. In the meantime, the DMK government had been dismissed on 31 January 1991 and Jayalalithaa had come to power. Although the interim award was published in the Union Gazette during her term, it produced no result. From 1991 to 1996, Jayalalithaa's government made no effort to implement it.

After the DMK returned to office in 1996, I repeatedly asked that the implementation scheme be finalised and gazetted without delay. I wrote to the Prime Minister on 9 July, 23 July, 29 September, 1 November and 6 November 1997, and met him personally on 27 July and 29 September. On 10 November 1997, the Tamil Nadu Government also filed a petition in the Supreme Court seeking a direction to the Union Government. I wrote again on 28 March, 6 April and 31 May 1998, and met the Prime Minister on 7 April, requesting that the draft scheme be finalised and gazetted.

On 21 July 1998, the Supreme Court observed: “Fifteen months have passed since the Union Government assured us that it would resolve the problem amicably. No mutually acceptable scheme has yet been framed. This state of affairs cannot be allowed to continue indefinitely.”

On 28 July 1998, Prime Minister Vajpayee invited me to a meeting at the Prime Minister's Office on 6 August to discuss the Cauvery issue. On 3 August, I convened a meeting of all party leaders in Tamil Nadu to decide whether I should attend. It resolved that I could participate. Jayalalithaa, however, issued a statement saying that I should not go.

At the end of the two-day meeting conducted by Prime Minister Vajpayee, an agreement was reached on 7 August 1998, under the DMK government, to implement the interim award. It provided that the 205 TMC of water specified in the interim order must be released and that a Cauvery River Authority comprising the Chief Ministers of the four riparian States would be formed under the Prime Minister's chairmanship.

Leaders including C. Subramaniam, G.K. Moopanar, Sankaraiah, R. Nallakannu, Abdul Samad, M.A. Latheef, Kumari Ananthan, Tindivanam Ramamurthy, R.M. Veerappan, G.A. Vadivelu, L. Ganesan, S. Thirunavukkarasar and Bishop Ezra Sargunam praised the agreement. So did the newspapers *The Hindu*, *The Indian Express*, *Dinamani*, *Viduthalai*, *Theekkathir*, *The Times of India* and *The Hindustan Times*, and the weeklies *Kalki*, *Kumudam*, *Saavi*, *Thuglak*, *Ananda Vikatan* and *Kungumam*. This is the history of what actually happened.

To cite one example, *The Hindustan Times*, under the headline “Karunanidhi Scores Diplomatic Victory,” wrote in substance: “The outcome of the Cauvery talks is a major political victory for Tamil Nadu Chief Minister M. Karunanidhi. Karnataka's agreement to implement the tribunal's interim award is itself a major victory. Since June 1990, when the tribunal directed Karnataka to supply Tamil Nadu 205 TMC of water, successive Karnataka governments had refused to accept the interim award. Mr Karunanidhi has made Karnataka accept it and has ensured that, when the tribunal delivers its final award on the quantity due to Tamil Nadu, 205 TMC will serve as the base figure. He has successfully broken the approach of the AIADMK and its allies that there should be no talks with Karnataka. He has thereby demonstrated that problems are better resolved through negotiation than through litigation. By not closing the door on talks, he has displayed great political maturity. By making the Prime Minister chairman of the Authority, he has secured a Union-level guarantee that Karnataka will implement the tribunal's award.”
That is not all. When leaders of every party and the press praised this historic agreement, did Jayalalithaa welcome it? No. She said that she rejected the settlement and would not accept it.

The person who enabled that settlement was BJP Prime Minister Vajpayee. At that time he was allied with the AIADMK. Even so, he invited me, leader of the DMK which was not part of that alliance, and reached an amicable settlement. Do you know what the AIADMK leader, who was part of the Union coalition, told reporters about the agreement?
“On the Cauvery issue, Vajpayee has fallen prey to Karunanidhi's stratagem. We reject the agreement the two have made together,” she said.

Jayalalithaa always nourishes the illusion that people will have forgotten the past and deceives herself. Concealing all this now, she pats herself on the back as though the interim award was implemented through her tireless efforts and as though institutions such as the Cauvery River Authority and Cauvery Monitoring Committee were created by her.

The Cauvery Tribunal's final award was delivered on 5 February 2007, when the DMK was in office. It was welcomed in statements by then Tamil Nadu Congress President M. Krishnasamy; Dravidar Kazhagam President, Tamilar Thalaivar K. Veeramani; Viduthalai Chiruthaigal Katchi General Secretary Thol. Thirumavalavan; CPI(M) State Secretary N. Varadarajan; CPI Tamil Nadu State Secretary D. Pandian; Cauvery Delta Irrigation Farmers' Association General Secretary Renganathan; and K.S. Natarajan of the Farmers and Workers Party.

Jayalalithaa, however, issued an AIADMK statement calling the final award “unacceptable.” She said: “Karunanidhi has failed to secure through the tribunal the full measure of justice due to Tamil Nadu in the Cauvery dispute. This final award is detrimental to Tamil Nadu. Accepting moral responsibility, the DMK government must resign.” As always, she demanded my resignation.
It is that very Jayalalithaa who now claims that she laboured to have the tribunal's final award published in the Union Gazette, and says the DMK made no effort towards it.

Even when the final award came, the DMK government did not choose to decide unilaterally as Jayalalithaa did. It convened all-party leaders' meetings on 19 February and 15 April 2007 before deciding its course. Now, instead of honouring democratic convention through such a meeting, the Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu acts as though whatever she thinks is the decision.
The truth, therefore, is that Jayalalithaa accepted neither the interim nor the final award of the Cauvery Tribunal. Did she at least accept the Cauvery River Authority?

The Cauvery River Authority was constituted and notified on 11 August 1998. Speaking in the Assembly on 2 April 2002, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa called it “a non-functioning Cauvery Authority” and “a toothless Authority,” and said: “We left the BJP alliance because we would not accept that Authority.”

At a Cabinet meeting chaired by her on 21 June 2002, she announced: “The Cauvery River Authority has no power and cannot effectively implement the tribunal's award. Tamil Nadu will therefore no longer participate in its meetings.” Without carefully considering the consequences, she made and announced the decision in her familiar impulsive manner.

When asked for my view, I said: “The Jayalalithaa government's decision to boycott the Authority is not wise. To obtain the water needed for the kuruvai paddy crop, all parties should be brought together to secure the Union Government's intervention and assistance. The dominant tendency of the AIADMK government is to reverse every decision taken under the DMK. Under the DMK, efforts were made to obtain water for the Cauvery irrigation region at the proper time. Under the AIADMK, the declaration is that it will not attend the Authority's meeting.”

Jayalalithaa boycotted the Cauvery Authority. On 2 July 2002, Cauvery delta farmers condemned her boycott and announced a hunger strike for 17 July. Even before that, on 10 July 2002, Karnataka Chief Minister S.M. Krishna had said: “Jayalalithaa did not ask me to release water into the Cauvery. It was Prime Minister Vajpayee who asked me to release 3 TMC.”

It was Jayalalithaa who declared in July 2002 that the Cauvery Authority would be boycotted. The next day she said, “We shall see if we are invited.” As for me, even when I was out of office, I wrote to Prime Minister Vajpayee on 5 July 2002 asking him to convene the Cauvery River Authority immediately. I wrote:

“The Cauvery River Authority, consisting of the Chief Ministers of Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Kerala and Pondicherry under your chairmanship, was established precisely to oversee Karnataka's compliance with the tribunal's order to release 205 TMC of water to Tamil Nadu. Since water has not been released in accordance with that order, farmers in the Cauvery delta are in great distress. Recognising the suffering of Cauvery-irrigated farmers, you asked the Karnataka Chief Minister to release 3 TMC to Tamil Nadu so that the problem might at least be eased. I convey my thanks for that request. The Karnataka Government, however, has refused it. I therefore request you to convene the Cauvery River Authority without delay and take immediate steps to implement the tribunal's order.”
In his reply of 10 July 2002 to my letter of 5 July seeking a meeting of the Authority, the Prime Minister said he would attend to the matter. The Cauvery River Authority was accordingly convened.

When Chief Minister Jayalalithaa attended the Authority meeting with the Prime Minister in Delhi on 27 August 2002, she did not remain and seek an amicable means of obtaining water. Because of her habitual haste and anger, she forgot the urgency of the issue, announced a walkout midway and left. The Authority met again on 29 November. Karnataka's Chief Minister arrived the previous day. Our Chief Minister Jayalalithaa said until the last minute that she would come, and then cancelled the Delhi trip itself. Who suffered the loss? Only Tamil Nadu's farmers!

Meanwhile, Supreme Court judges told counsel for the Tamil Nadu Government: “Chief Minister Jayalalithaa's letter to the Prime Minister saying that she had no confidence in the Cauvery River Authority chaired by Prime Minister Vajpayee is highly objectionable. That defamatory letter must be withdrawn within four days.” Tamil Nadu's counsel, K.K. Venugopal, told the judges: “Tamil Nadu has complete confidence in the Prime Minister and in the Cauvery River Authority functioning under his leadership. Chief Minister Jayalalithaa will very soon write again to Prime Minister Vajpayee expressing confidence in the Authority and withdrawing her earlier letter.”

Accordingly, in her letter to the Prime Minister on 16 November 2002, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa wrote that she held him personally in great esteem and, setting aside everything she had said earlier, that she also had confidence in the Cauvery Authority.

Chief Minister Jayalalithaa continues to claim that Karunanidhi took no action and did not lift even a splinter over the Cauvery issue because she trusts that no one will remember all these historical facts.
To summarise the ways in which Jayalalithaa disregarded the welfare of delta farmers at every stage of the Cauvery water dispute:
Even after the tribunal issued its interim award, it was Jayalalithaa who said water would come only if the Karnataka Government chose to release it.
When the Union Government sought an explanation on implementing the interim order, it was Jayalalithaa who replied after a delay of nine months.

Although she was in power for five years from 1991 to 1996, Jayalalithaa made no effort to implement the interim order. In a petition submitted to Prime Minister V.P. Singh on behalf of the AIADMK, it was Jayalalithaa who wrote that the Cauvery agreement had expired in 1974.
It was Jayalalithaa who cancelled her participation and failed to attend the Cauvery Authority meeting on 29 November 2002. The list could go on.
Do you understand now who betrayed Tamil Nadu on the Cauvery issue?
With affection,
M.K.
